item.title: SCP Classifications
item.content:encoded:
Well, Euclid does not mean he did something wrong or is dangerous. It simply means he has the ability to breach containment, become violent and/or may cause casualties. Just because something is Safe doesn't mean it's harmless and can't kill. It is just easier to contain and not very likely/able to breach containment.

This is all explained here.
More general:

As a note, any SCP that's autonomous, sentient and/or sapient is generally classified as Euclid, due to the inherent unpredictability of an object that can act or think on its own.

There is also the Locked Box Test (also described on the linked page.)

But I do agree that it is a pretty good and very SCP-esque article! If it wasn't so old (and thus probably won't recieve any updates), I'd also love some testing procedures/results and maybe crosstests with other SCPs like SCP-1056.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Definite +1 from me. A guy with a planet in his stomach definitely looks the part to be an SCP but his passive attitude towards it is what takes the cake in my opinion. He's so chill that he's happy to play a round of chess with his captors which I find hilarious. Even more hilarious is the fact they designated him Euclid when this guy seems to have done nothing wrong. It would be cool to maybe include a transcript of a conversation had over chess to help characterize him a bit more. I'm also kind of interested in the relationship this guy has with the planet and vis versa. Does he somehow provide the planet and/or it inhabitants with "sustenance"? Does the existence of the planet imbue him with any special powers? This could be an opportunity to play up the foundation's cruelty and/or recklessness by having them do something to piss him off and something drastic happens thus justifying the Euclid designation. Of course, the article is pretty good as it is and, given the high rating it has, I'm sure it won't be changing anytime soon. This is just my two cents on how it could be made better.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: "humminoid"

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I love how casual this entire article's tone is. Not only is the guy not surprised about the planet, but the Foundation does not treat this as a shocking anomaly in any way. A person has a planet in his chest, business as usual. Really shows that the priorities are where they say they are. No needless research resources poured into something that does not impact our world.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Why are so many people concerned with changing the object class, Just because he doesn't want to escape containment hes still humminoid, You don't know what will happen if you lock him up

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
007, planetary bond.
I love this guy. He doesn’t do much, but the Idea of being in a symbiotic relationship with a tiny planet is so fun. Hello, little people!
Sure, it could use a bit more material, but I like the concept, I like the execution, it deserves the love it gets. Especially since people are still writing tiny planet skips to this day, it makes sense to pay repsects to the first one.
+1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Should the planet itself be SCP-007 instead of the "subject?" The Subject isn't just subject to the planet. He has anomalous qualities on his own with the whole surviving without tissue thing.
I know it's an old article, but wouldn't the format normally have the human be SCP-007, and the planet be a facet of him?

item.title: Re: It's pretty meh
item.content:encoded:
Sadly, it's missing a Site where is it contained. Site 19, as usual?

A Site doesn't have to be specified in the Procedures. And there's something to be said about putting all your eggs in one basket where unpredictable, potentially dangerous objects are concerned.

In this case, where it's contained at isn't as important as what tye object is, as that's the focus of the article.

item.title: It's pretty meh
item.content:encoded:
Nothing wrong with being meh. It's OK and I upvoted.
Sadly, it's missing a Site where is it contained. Site 19, as usual?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: So, do the small people in the mini-earth know they are in a man´s belly? I mean, how far is the "Surface" of that planet to the Subject´s flesh? Can they even see it?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The original author of this hasn't been active since 2009 so I don't foresee them returning to change this any time soon.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Maybe the author could do an update on this? Seems like a cool concept.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
if it hasn't done anything inexplicably dangerous, why is it classified as Euclid?

Object classes are based on difficulty of containment, not potential for danger.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: A lot could be done with this article in the long run, but if it hasn't done anything inexplicably dangerous, why is it classified as Euclid?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: That was indeed me as a new user, although I skipped -001 at first because there was a bunch of stuff. Thankfully, I've read all of the -001's now.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Should probably be "SCP-007-A" for the guy and "SCP-007-B" for the planet, since they're clearly both anomalous. I like the idea, though. Some development would be nice (maybe some additional information about the two intelligent species on the planet? Are they at war? What do they look like?) but it's not really necessary, especially since it's already at +135.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Take a different IQ test and it might change.

Not that IQ really means much, though.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Hm, 007's IQ is 128, whereas mine is 127, peculiar.

item.title: It feels unfinished, somehow...
item.content:encoded:
There's nothing glaringly wrong with it, but it leaves me with a bunch of questions. It reads like a lazy report. If I were senior staff reading this report, I would demand it be finished. "Dammit, why DON'T we know how he swallows water? Are we out of tracking devices? And X-ray machines?? And MRIs? Sloppy work, Henderson. I want this project documented - fully documented, by tomorrow morning."
Of course, if you don't want to REVEAL why the Foundation doesn't know or if it knows and isn't telling, that's fine, but that's the exact reason why data gets redacted, not just un-addressed.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I found this as a rather dull SCP for a while, too.

Then I realized a small little detail: The fact that 007 is always requesting a computer with internet.

Think about a few facts. This is a guy who has entire civilizations living on a planet located in replacement of his stomach. This is a guy who may or may not be able to communicate with said civilizations as a result of being located in replacement of his stomach. And this is a guy requesting access to one of the largest databases of information on mankind that has ever been assembled.

I dunno about you guys, but I smell nothing but trouble brewing here. And that's really what captures my attention.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Not sure if it classifies as a good reason, but many new users will start reading at 001 and go on from there. This being in the 7th slot means it's bound to get its share of "ehrmagerd, so kewl!" votes before they find the higher-quality stuff.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I personally think it's kind of neat, but I do agree that there's no good reason for it to be +95.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is such a "just sort of there" SCP, I honestly can't believe it's rated as high as it is.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I'm having trouble visualizing the Subject, as 60cm diameter is a quite large sphere (a basketball has around 25cm diameter).

Does the planet reside in a cavity with only one opening? His torso would be very deformed to house it.

Or is his body like Street Fighter IV's Seth, his torso and legs not in contact?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: When the little people living on the planet start trying to take over the rest of his body or leave their planet, let me know.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I agree with this, but it's sitting at +69, so it doesn't really need changing.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The overuse of "subject" in this article makes me twitch. Should there not be some uses of The subject? Or other words? I get that it's ancient but that bugs me : /

item.title: Re: Down with slash
item.content:encoded:
When he wakes up, he'll believe he has a cognizant argument, but that will flutter away as consciousness firms up.

Mann, quit messing with my control group, it's unseemly.

item.title: Re: Down with slash
item.content:encoded: Noted. I'm working out a semi-cogent argument for further deslashments, but that's for another thread (and for after some sleep).

item.title: Re: Down with slash
item.content:encoded:
I disagree.

Not with the change to the object class, that's spot on. I just object to the idea of you having free will. It goes against the grain.

item.title: Down with slash
item.content:encoded:
Dual-classes SCPs have long been a pet peeve of mine, so I'm gonna do something about it. This guy I changed from Safe/Euclid to just Euclid since, in my mind, both sentient creatures and sentient collectives default to Euclid, and nothing in the article leads me to believe the Foundation is treating 007 as a Safe object.

Note that this is a unilateral decision, and as such is subject to mod/admin override. It's also, as always, subect to commentary from the hoi polloi.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I wonder what would happen if the tiny civilization invented space travel….

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I just like the fact that he's just like " Meh I woke up with this one day but everything's cool". Could be funnier (that is if you're writing a funny one, which I hope to god you are) if you added more personality to the people on the planet.

item.title: interaction?
item.content:encoded:
So, it is established that the planet with does not make any physical contact with its host. But does that preclude him actually touching it, or interacting with it. If that were me, I'd have done poked at it a little and started making a few landscaping changes (while trying not to squish inhabitants).

Also, if the denizens of the planet are sentient, and have 15th century tech, then they probably have discovered astronomy. At the very least, they are studying the sky above them and wondering about the host and the other giant creatures that walk by their heavens.

Probably not anything to add to the scip, but certainly material for a tale or two.

I do rather like this one by the way. It’s the combination of weird thing happening to Joe Blow, coupled with him no longer existing in official records.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I really like this one, it always sticks with me and pops into my mind from time to time. Really reminds me of something you'd see in a tool video.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I just read that part (it's from "The Last Continent", and the Bursar is the one affected by it), and it has nothing to do with either of these: The planets are circling around his head as a metaphor for the Bursar's savantry, and aren't even solid. I wouldn't mind some more Discworld references for the Foundation, other than one line in the 914 log.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I would like that idea, if it where not for the fact that it would constantly remind me of Discworld, there is a reference there of a wizard who was exposed to a high magic environment "coming down with planets."

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
See, and I prefer this version, but I do like the idea that the two may be connected.

What if the phenomena of small planets forming in people's bodies is a disease? Someone skilled at writing could decomission both of these and merge them into a single article.

item.title: Re:
item.content:encoded: Well, it's a matter that this came first. In fact, it's quite old. Buuut, I do like 756 more than this.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
http://scp-wiki.wikidot.com/scp-756

It has the same "mini planet" concept as this, but I personally find 756 much more interesting. Putting the planet in some random guy's body adds to the WTF factor, but not in any significant way.
